label: Trigonometry Application
claim: Trigonometry is used to determine the height and distance of different items without measuring them.
evidence: students in Class 10 will learn how to use trigonometry to determine the height and distance of different items without having to measure them.

label: Historical Use of Trigonometry
claim: Trigonometry was once used by astronomers to compute the distance between the planets, the Earth and stars.
evidence: Trigonometry was once used by astronomers to compute the distance between the planets, the Earth and stars.

label: Geographical Use of Trigonometry
claim: In geography and navigation, trigonometry is most frequently used to identify the latitude and longitude of a specified position.
evidence: In geography and navigation, trigonometry is most frequently used to identify the latitude and longitude of a specified position.

label: Line of Sight
claim: The line of sight is the line drawn from the eye of the observer to the point on the object viewed by the observer.
evidence: Line of sight is the line drawn from the eye of the observer to the point on the object viewed by the observer.

label: Horizontal Level
claim: The horizontal level is the horizontal line through the eye of the observer.
evidence: the horizontal level is the horizontal line through the eye of the observer.

label: Angle of Elevation
claim: The angle of elevation is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal level and is relevant for objects above the horizontal level.
evidence: the angle of elevation is relevant for objects above the horizontal level. It is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal level.

label: Angle of Elevation
claim: The angle of elevation is used to find heights.
evidence: Angle of elevation To know more about the Angle of elevation,[visit here](https://byjus.com/maths/angle-of-elevation/).

label: Angle of Depression
claim: The angle of depression is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal level for objects below the horizontal.
evidence: the angle of depression is relevant for objects below the horizontal level. It is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal level.

label: Calculating Heights and Distances
claim: Trigonometric ratios can be used to calculate heights and distances.
evidence: To calculate heights and distances, we can make use of trigonometric ratios.

label: Solving Trigonometry Problems
claim: To solve problems involving heights and distances, follow these steps: Draw a line diagram, mark knowns/unknowns, and use trigonometric ratios to find unknowns.
evidence: Step 1: Draw a line diagram corresponding to the problem. Step 2: Mark all known heights, distances and angles and denote unknown lengths by variables. Step 3: Use the values of various trigonometric ratios of the angles to obtain the unknown lengths from the known lengths.

label: Trigonometric Ratios
claim: Trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan, cot, cosec, sec) are used to relate angles to side lengths.
evidence: Use the values of various trigonometric ratios of the angles to obtain the unknown lengths from the known lengths.

label: Parallax Method
claim: The parallax method is used to measure large distances of celestial bodies.
evidence: Large distances can be measured by the parallax method.

label: Parallax Angle
claim: The parallax angle is half the angle between two lines of sight when an object is viewed from two different positions.
evidence: The parallax angle is half the angle between two lines of sight when an object is viewed from two different positions.

label: Trigonometry in Height Measurement (Example 1)
claim: Trigonometry can be used to find the length of a string when the height of an object and the angle of inclination are known (using sine).
evidence: In right triangle ABC, sin 60° = AB/AC √3/2 = 60/AC AC = (60 ×2)√/3 = (120 ×√3)/(√3 ×√3) = (120√3)/3 = 40√3 Therefore, the length of the string is 40√3 m.

label: Trigonometry in Height Measurement (Example 2)
claim: Trigonometry can be used to find the height of a TV tower and the width of a canal given two angles of elevation from different points.
evidence: From another point 20 m away from this point on the line joining this point to the foot of the tower, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower is 30° as shown in the figure. Find the height of the tower and the width of the canal.

label: Trigonometry in Right Triangles (Tangent)
claim: In a right triangle, the tangent of an angle is the ratio of the length of the side opposite the angle to the length of the side adjacent to the angle.
evidence: tan 30° = AB/BD 1/√3 = AB/(20 + BC)

label: Application: Finding Unknown Lengths using Tangent
claim: Trigonometric ratios like tangent can be used to find unknown lengths in real-world scenarios, such as finding the height of a tower or the width of a canal.
evidence: Therefore, the height of the tower is 10√3 m, and the width of the canal is 10 m.

label: Application: Solving Problems with Angles of Depression/Elevation
claim: Trigonometry is used to solve problems involving angles of elevation and depression, such as finding the distance between two objects from the top of a tower.
evidence: From the top of a tower h m high, the angles of depression of two objects, which are in line with the foot of the tower are αand β(β > α). Find the distance between the two objects.

label: Application: Solving Problems with Ladders and Walls
claim: Trigonometry can be applied to find the height of a vertical wall when the length of a ladder and the angle it makes with the wall are known.
evidence: A ladder 20 metres long just reaches the top of a vertical wall. If the ladder makes an angle of 30° with the wall, find the height of the wall.

label: Trigonometry Applications
claim: Trigonometry has applications.
evidence: some-application-of-trigonometry-for-class-10

label: Trigonometry Application
claim: Trigonometry has real-life uses.
evidence: Real-Life Uses of Trigonometry in Class 10 Maths

label: Trigonometry application
claim: Trigonometry can be used to find unknown heights or lengths when given an angle and a known side length.
evidence: Evaluate the height of the coconut tree, if the angle formed by the rope with the ground level is 30°.

label: Sine function in right triangle
claim: In a right-angled triangle, the sine of an angle is the ratio of the length of the opposite side to the length of the hypotenuse (sin $	heta$ = opposite/hypotenuse).
evidence: sin 30° = AB/AC ½= h/20 (since sin 30° = ½)

label: Calculating height from rope
claim: If a rope is tied to a pole and forms an angle with the ground, the height of an object on the pole can be calculated using trigonometry.
evidence: A villager is climbing a coconut tree using a 20 m long rope, which is tightly tied from the top of a vertical pole to the ground. Evaluate the height of the coconut tree, if the angle formed by the rope with the ground level is 30°.

label: Finding slide length using trigonometry
claim: Trigonometry can be used to find the length of a slide when the height and the angle of inclination are known.
evidence: Find out the length of the slide in both the cases?

label: Air balloon string application
claim: The inclination of a string tied to an air balloon above the ground can be used with trigonometry to relate the height and the string length.
evidence: An air balloon is flying above the ground at a height of 60 m. The string joined to the balloon is temporarily tied to a point on the ground. The inclination of the string with the ground level is 60°.

label: Applications of Trigonometry
claim: The chapter primarily focuses on using trigonometry to solve real-world problems involving heights and distances.
evidence: This chapter primarily focuses on using trigonometry to solve real-world problems involving heights and distances.

label: Line of Sight
claim: The line of sight is the imaginary line from an observer's eye to the object being viewed.
evidence: Line of Sight: The imaginary line from an observer's eye to the object being viewed.

label: Angle of Elevation
claim: The angle of elevation is the angle formed by the line of sight and the horizontal when the object is above the horizontal level.
evidence: Angle of Elevation: The angle formed by the line of sight and the horizontal when the object is above the horizontal level.

label: Angle of Depression
claim: The angle of depression is the angle formed by the line of sight and the horizontal when the object is below the horizontal level.
evidence: Angle of Depression: The angle formed by the line of sight and the horizontal when the object is below the horizontal level.

label: Trigonometric Ratios Application
claim: Students learn to apply trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan) to calculate unknown heights or distances by forming right-angled triangles.
evidence: Students learn to apply trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan) to calculate unknown heights or distances by forming right-angled triangles.

label: Architecture and Engineering Application
claim: Trigonometry is used in architecture and engineering to calculate the height of structures like buildings, towers, and bridges without direct measurement.
evidence: Calculating the height of buildings, towers, and bridges without direct measurement.

label: Navigation Application
claim: Trigonometry is used in navigation (aviation and shipping) to determine locations and distances using angles.
evidence: Used in aviation and shipping to determine locations and distances using angles.

label: Angle of Elevation Definition
claim: The angle of elevation is formed when an observer looks upward from a horizontal line to see an object, being the angle between the horizontal line and the line of sight to the object above.
evidence: The angle of elevation is formed when an observer has to look upward from a horizontal line to see an object. It is the angle between the horizontal line and the line of sight to the object above.

label: Angle of Depression Definition
claim: The angle of depression is formed when an observer looks downward from a horizontal line to see an object, being the angle between the horizontal line and the line of sight to the object below.
evidence: The angle of depression is formed when an observer has to look downward from a horizontal line to see an object. It is the angle between the horizontal line and the line of sight to the object below.

label: Trigonometric Ratio Definitions (SOH-CAH-TOA)
claim: The basic trigonometric ratios for a right-angled triangle are: Sine (sin θ) = Opposite / Hypotenuse, Cosine (cos θ) = Adjacent / Hypotenuse, and Tangent (tan θ) = Opposite / Adjacent.
evidence: Sine (sin θ) = Length of the side Opposite to angle θ / Length of the Hypotenuse; Cosine (cos θ) = Length of the side Adjacent to angle θ / Length of the Hypotenuse; Tangent (tan θ) = Length of the side Opposite to angle θ / Length of the side Adjacent

label: Choosing the Correct Ratio (Tangent Rule)
claim: Use the tangent (tan θ) ratio when a problem involves the Opposite and Adjacent sides.
evidence: Use tan θ if your problem involves the Opposite and Adjacent sides.

label: SOH rule
claim: Use sin θ if your problem involves the Opposite side and the Hypotenuse.
evidence: Use sin θ if your problem involves the Opposite side and the Hypotenuse.

label: CAH rule
claim: Use cos θ if your problem involves the Adjacent side and the Hypotenuse.
evidence: Use cos θ if your problem involves the Adjacent side and the Hypotenuse.

label: Architectural application
claim: Architects use trigonometry to determine the required height of a building based on its shadow.
evidence: an architect might use the angle of elevation to determine the required height of a building based on the shadow it will cast at a certain time of day.

label: Engineering application
claim: Engineers use trigonometry to calculate things like the slope of a ramp, the span and height of bridge arches, and forces on a structure.
evidence: Engineers use these concepts to calculate the slope of a ramp for accessibility, the span and height of bridge arches, and the forces acting on a structure.

label: Standard angle importance
claim: Standard angles like 30°, 45°, and 60° are important because they give exact, rationalised values for sin, cos, and tan.
evidence: These angles yield exact, rationalised values for sin, cos, and tan (e.g., sin 30° = 1/2, tan 45° = 1).

label: Misconception/Strategy
claim: Using standard trigonometric values helps students solve problems manually by focusing on the problem setup rather than complex calculations.
evidence: Using these standard values allows students to solve problems manually without needing a calculator, focusing their efforts on understanding the problem setup and application of the correct ratio, rather than complex calculations.

label: Trigonometry Application Focus
claim: Applications of trigonometry rely more on logical application and visualization than memorization of complex formulas.
evidence: this one relies on a few core concepts, making it less about memorisation and more about logical application and visualisation.

label: Trigonometry Core Concept
claim: Trigonometry applications are based on a few core concepts.
evidence: this one relies on a few core concepts

label: Related Geometry Concept
claim: The topic includes concepts like Curved Surface, which has definitions, formulas, and real-life examples.
evidence: Curved Surface: Definition, Formula & Real-Life Examples

label: Applications of Trigonometry
claim: Trigonometry is used to find heights and distances that cannot be measured directly.
evidence: Applications of Trigonometry deal with using trigonometric ratios to find heights and distances that cannot be measured directly.

label: Angle of elevation
claim: The angle of elevation is the angle above the horizontal when looking up at an object.
evidence: Angle of elevation: The angle above the horizontal when looking up at an object.

label: Angle of depression
claim: The angle of depression is the angle below the horizontal when looking down at an object.
evidence: Angle of depression: The angle below the horizontal when looking down at an object.

label: Application of Trigonometry
claim: Trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan) are used to calculate heights of buildings, towers, mountains, widths of rivers, and distances between objects.
evidence: Using these angles along with trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan), we can calculate heights of buildings, towers, mountains, widths of rivers, and distances between objects.

label: Tangent Ratio Usage
claim: The tangent ratio is most often used because the typical setup involves a vertical height and a horizontal distance (opposite and adjacent sides).
evidence: Most problems use the tangent ratio because the typical setup involves a vertical height and a horizontal distance, which are the opposite and adjacent sides respectively.

label: Angle of Elevation
claim: The angle of elevation is the angle formed between the horizontal line of sight and the line of sight to an object above the horizontal level.
evidence: The angle formed between the horizontal line of sight and the line of sight to an object above the horizontal level.

label: Angle of Depression
claim: The angle of depression is the angle formed between the horizontal line of sight and the line of sight to an object below the horizontal level.
evidence: The angle formed between the horizontal line of sight and the line of sight to an object below the horizontal level.

label: Elevation and Depression Relationship
claim: The angle of elevation from A to B equals the angle of depression from B to A because of alternate interior angles (since the horizontals at A and B are parallel).
evidence: Angle of elevation from A to B = Angle of depression from B to A(alternate interior angles, since the horizontals at A and B are parallel)

label: Tangent Ratio
claim: The tangent ratio (tan theta) is calculated as Opposite/Adjacent, which is used most often because problems typically involve a height (opposite) and a horizontal distance (adjacent).
evidence: tan theta = Opposite/Adjacent = Height/Distances Most commonly used ratio: - tan theta is used most often because problems typically involve a height (opposite) and a horizontal distance (adjacent).

label: Right Triangle Setup
claim: To solve height and distance problems, identify the right triangle formed by the vertical height (opposite side), the horizontal distance (adjacent side), and the line of sight (hypotenuse).
evidence: Identify the right triangle formed by: - The vertical height (opposite side) - The horizontal distance (adjacent side) - The line of sight (hypotenuse)

label: Angle of Elevation/Depression Measurement
claim: The angle of elevation or depression is always measured from the horizontal, never from the vertical.
evidence: The angle of elevation/depression is always measured from the horizontal, never from the vertical.

label: Measurement Reference Point
claim: When the observer has a specific height (e.g., a person 1.5 m tall), measurements are from eye level, not from the ground.
evidence: When the observer has a specific height (e.g., a person 1.5 m tall), measurements are from eye level, not from the ground.

label: Finding Height from Ground
claim: To find the height (h) from the ground when given the angle of elevation and horizontal distance (d), use the formula tan theta = h/d.
evidence: Find: height using tan theta = h/d.

label: Finding Distance
claim: To find the distance (d) when given the angle of elevation and height (h), use the formula d = h/tan theta.
evidence: Find: distance using d = h/tan theta.

label: Core Trigonometric Ratio
claim: The tangent ratio (tan theta) is most often used in height and distance problems because it connects height and distance.
evidence: Choose the ratio — tan theta is used most often (connects height and distance).

label: Angle of Depression
claim: The angle of depression from A to B equals the angle of elevation from B to A.
evidence: The angle of depression from A to B equals the angle of elevation from B to A.

label: Angle of Depression Measurement
claim: When the problem says 'angle of depression from top of a tower,' the angle is at the top, measured from the horizontal downward.
evidence: When the problem says "angle of depression from top of a tower," the angle is at the top, measured from the horizontal downward.

label: Trigonometry for Height (Tangent)
claim: To find the height (h) when given the horizontal distance (d) and the angle of elevation, use the formula tan(angle) = h/d.

label: Trigonometry for Length (Sine)
claim: To find the length of a string (hypotenuse) when given the height and the angle with the horizontal, use the formula sin(angle) = height/length of string.
evidence: Using: sin 30 = height/length of string - 1/2 = 60/length - Length = 120 m

label: Numerical Constant
claim: Use sqrt(3) = 1.732 for final numerical answers when asked.
evidence: Use sqrt(3) = 1.732 for final numerical answers when asked.

label: Trigonometric ratio tan
claim: The tangent function (tan) relates the opposite side (height) to the adjacent side (distance).
evidence: tan 60 = h/d

label: Finding distance from nearer point
claim: If the angle of elevation is 60 degrees from a nearer point, the distance (d) can be found using the formula d = h/sqrt(3).
evidence: tan 60 = h/d → sqrt(3) = h/d → d = h/sqrt(3)

label: Finding distance from farther point
claim: If the angle of elevation is 30 degrees from a farther point, the distance (d) is related by the equation 1/sqrt(3) = h/(d + 40).

label: Adjusting for observer height
claim: When calculating angles of elevation from an observer's eyes, the effective height used in the calculation is the total height minus the observer's height.
evidence: Effective height = 30 - 1.5 = 28.5 m (from the boy's eye level to the top).

label: Application: Flagstaff height calculation
claim: Trigonometry can be used to find the height of an object (flagstaff) given the angles of elevation to its top and bottom from a point on the ground.
evidence: Find the height of the flagstaff.

label: Application: River width calculation
claim: Trigonometry can be used to find the width of a river when two different angles of elevation to an object on the opposite bank are known from two different points on the near bank.
evidence: Find the width of the river.

label: Trigonometric Ratio Application
claim: Trigonometric ratios can be used to calculate distances or heights in real-world problems.
evidence: Using: tan 60 = 28.5/d - sqrt(3) = 28.5/d - d = 28.5/sqrt(3) = 28.5 sqrt(3)/3 = 9.5 sqrt(3) = 16.45 m Answer: Distance = 9.5 sqrt(3) m (approximately 16.45 m).

label: Angle of Depression Problem
claim: Trigonometry can be used to find the height of an object (like a tower) when the angles of depression from a higher point are known.
evidence: From the top of a 120 m high building, the angles of depression of the top and bottom of a tower are 30 degrees and 60 degrees respectively. Find the height of the tower. Solution: ... Answer: Height of the tower = 80 m.

label: Shadow Problem Application
claim: Trigonometry can solve problems involving the height of an object using the relationship between the angle of elevation of the sun and the length of its shadow.
evidence: The shadow of a tower, when the angle of elevation of the sun is 45 degrees, is found to be 10 m longer than when it was 60 degrees. Find the height of the tower. Solution: ... Answer: Height = 5(3 + sqrt(3)) m (approximately 23.66 m).

label: Relating Angles and Distances (Shadow Example)
claim: In a shadow problem, the height of an object can be found by setting up equations based on two different angles of elevation.
evidence: When angle = 60: tan 60 = h/d → d = h/sqrt(3) When angle = 45: tan 45 = h/(d + 10) → h = d + 10 Substituting: ...
